text: Three 8mm trocars are placed in the left lateral abdomen, establishing access points for the robotic instruments during the hernia repair procedure.
The robotic system is docked, providing a clear view of the hernia located at the umbilicus.
The surgeon initiates dissection into the preperitoneal space, starting from a cephalad position toward the head and progressing caudad toward the feet.
The surgeon continues to develop the preperitoneal space, focusing on the central preperitoneal fat pad and progressing toward the hernia sac located at the umbilicus.
The surgeon reduces the hernia sac at the umbilicus, carefully dissecting it away from the overlying umbilical stalk using robotic instruments.
The surgeon continues the dissection toward the right lateral side of the abdominal wall, transitioning from the preperitoneal fat pad to the lateral peritoneal layer.
The surgeon carefully handles the thin peritoneal layer during the preperitoneal approach to ensure its integrity, which is crucial for excluding the mesh from contact with the abdominal viscera.
The surgeon continues the circumferential dissection, creating a large prepared space for the placement of a synthetic mesh with sufficient overlap to minimize the risk of hernia recurrence.
The surgeon completes the dissection, finalizing the preparation of the surgical site for hernia repair.

The surgeon introduces a fascial closure suture and uses a ruler to measure the hernia defect, which is approximately 2.5 centimeters wide and 2 centimeters long. After measurement, the ruler is removed and the fascia is closed.
The surgeon uses a PDS absorbable unidirectional barbed suture to maintain tension on the fascia during closure, ensuring secure approximation of the tissue edges.
The surgeon removes the fascial closure suture and introduces new sutures to anchor the synthetic mesh and close the peritoneum.
The surgeon introduces a polyester ProGrip mesh, cut into a thirteen-centimeter diameter circle, ensuring wide overlap over the hernia defect to minimize recurrence risk.

The surgeon anchors the polyester ProGrip mesh at the three o'clock, six o'clock, nine o'clock, and twelve o'clock positions using vicryl sutures, with an additional suture placed in the center of the mesh.

The surgeon uses a polyester ProGrip mesh, which is visible in the surgical field. Additionally, a mid-weight polypropylene mesh is mentioned as frequently used, both being synthetic and permanent meshes.
The surgeon closes the peritoneal flap using a unidirectional barb suture, ensuring the mesh is completely excluded from the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon inspects the peritoneum for holes and closes a hole within the reduced hernia sac using interrupted vicryl sutures to prevent mesh contact with the abdominal viscera.
The surgeon places the omentum back over the abdominal viscera, ensuring it covers the underlying structures. All needles and sutures are then carefully removed from the abdominal cavity.
